metadata.note: Output used as-is without manual revision or selection.

causal_texts: The glass slipped from her hand. It fell to the floor. It broke into many pieces. She swept them up carefully.

Dark clouds gathered above the picnic tables during the warm afternoon. Heavy rain started suddenly and soaked the plates and blankets. Everyone grabbed the food and ran toward the wooden shelter. They waited there quietly until the rain slowed and sunshine returned.
His alarm never rang on that busy school morning. He woke late and dressed in a rush. He ran outside with untied shoes and a half packed bag. The bus pulled away just before he reached the corner.

Maria planted a small seed in the soft garden soil. She watered the spot every afternoon after school. A green sprout pushed through the dirt one week later. She tied the thin stem gently to a wooden stick.

The cat noticed a butterfly resting near the backyard fence. It leaped through the flowers and missed the bright wings. Its paws struck the wooden boards with a dull thump. It returned indoors and slept on the living room rug.

The power suddenly went out after our family finished dinner. We found two candles inside the crowded kitchen drawer. Their warm light filled the room with a steady glow. We read quietly for an hour and then slept early.

Sam mixed flour, eggs, milk, and sugar in a bowl. He poured the smooth batter into a round pan. The cake baked until the top turned evenly golden brown. He cooled it fully and spread chocolate frosting across it.

My phone rang loudly during the quiet morning meeting. I stepped outside the room and answered quickly. My friend invited me to lunch at the corner cafe. I opened my calendar and saved the plan immediately.

Ben walked into the house wearing muddy shoes from outside. Brown prints spread across the clean hallway floor behind him. He grabbed a mop, a bucket, and warm water. The hallway looked bright and spotless again before dinner started.

Elena noticed a slow leak under the kitchen sink. She turned the water valve off with both hands. She called a plumber from the hallway using her phone. She mopped the growing puddle before it reached the cabinets.

The bicycle tire went flat while Jay crossed downtown traffic. He walked the bike slowly to a nearby repair shop. A mechanic patched the small hole and checked the rim. Jay rode home before the evening sky turned dark.

The soup bubbled too hard on the hot stove. Hot broth spilled over the pot and covered the burner. Nina lowered the heat and wiped the mess carefully. She served lunch once the soup settled and stopped splashing.

The train stopped far outside the station during rush hour. Passengers waited quietly inside the warm car for news. An announcement finally explained the delay over the speaker. Everyone boarded another train twenty minutes later and continued home.

A child dropped his ice cream cone beside the bench. The scoop landed upside down on the dusty path. He cried until his father returned from the kiosk. His father bought another cone and wiped his sticky hands.

A painter opened a fresh blue can near the wall. The ladder shook suddenly under his left foot. Paint splashed across the wooden floor in a wide arc. He wiped the mess and restarted painting the wall carefully.

A strong wind scattered the student's papers across the courtyard. She chased the loose pages between benches and flower pots. She gathered every sheet into a blue plastic folder. She clipped the folder shut before the bell rang.

Lena studied every chapter carefully during the long weekend. She answered each test question with steady focus. The teacher returned the graded papers after lunch. Lena smiled widely when she saw her excellent score.

The camera battery died just before the sunset began. Omar plugged the charger into the wall beside his desk. A green light stayed on for nearly an hour. He returned outside and captured the orange evening sky.

A button fell from my shirt collar before work. I searched the drawer for thread, scissors, and a needle. I stitched the button back into place with care. I wore the shirt comfortably for the rest of the day.

The dog slipped through the open gate during breakfast. We heard loud barking near the small pond. My brother found it there and clipped on the leash. We walked home together before dark clouds covered the park.

My laptop froze while I was finishing an important report. I restarted the system with nervous hands and crossed my fingers. The recovery screen loaded my saved draft after a minute. I finished the report and emailed it before noon.

Snow covered the driveway before anyone in the house woke. Dad pushed the shovel through the heavy drifts slowly. A narrow path reached the front steps and mailbox. We carried groceries inside without slipping on the ice.

The recipe needed cinnamon for the cookie dough tonight. I checked the shelf twice and found none anywhere. I walked to the corner store through light rain. The cookies smelled perfect after the fresh cinnamon was added.

The email attachment bounced back right after Priya pressed send. She compressed the file into a smaller folder. She sent the message again to her manager. Her manager replied that the file opened without any trouble.

The kite string twisted above the field in gusty wind. Leo stopped running and pulled the kite lower. He loosened the knots carefully with patient fingers. The kite lifted smoothly again and floated over the trees.

My shoelace snapped during the walk home from school. I stopped beside a quiet doorway and looked down. A friend offered a spare lace from his backpack. I threaded it through and kept walking toward home.

The soccer ball rolled into the street after a hard kick. Maya waited for the passing cars to clear. She hurried out, grabbed the ball, and ran back. She returned to the field and kept playing happily.

I spilled orange juice on my notebook during breakfast. The pages curled and stuck together under the wet stain. I dried them outside in the afternoon sun. I copied the blurry notes again that same evening.

We reached the museum after lunch on a warm Saturday. A sign near the entrance announced an early closing. We visited the nearby park instead and watched ducks. We returned to the museum the next morning.

The traffic light stopped working at the crowded noon crossing. A police officer walked into the intersection with confidence. Drivers followed her hand signals and slowed down. Cars moved again without blocking the center lane.

The curtain suddenly fell from the rod above the window. Tom picked up the rod, rings, and fallen screws. He tightened the brackets firmly against the wall. The curtain hung straight again before the evening breeze arrived.

The fish tank water looked cloudy and dull this morning. Sara replaced the dirty filter cartridge with a clean one. She poured fresh water into the tank very slowly. The fish swam calmly around the green plants again.
A large parcel arrived at our door before breakfast ended. Nina signed for the box with a black pen. She opened it and found a new desk lamp. She assembled the lamp beside her books that afternoon.

The bread stuck inside the toaster during my usual breakfast. I unplugged the toaster and waited for it to cool. The metal springs relaxed after a quiet minute. I lifted the slice out and ate breakfast normally.

A bee entered the classroom through the open side window. The students moved away from the sharp buzzing sound. The teacher opened the far window much wider. The bee flew outside and disappeared above the garden.

I forgot my keys inside the apartment after breakfast. The door clicked shut behind me in the hallway. I called my neighbor and explained the problem quickly. He brought the spare key downstairs and let me in.
The first pancake burned black in the hot pan. Carla lowered the flame and moved the pan aside. She cleaned the surface with a folded paper towel. The next pancakes came out soft, round, and golden.

The garden hose cracked near the nozzle during watering. Water sprayed wildly across the tomato bed and path. Ben turned the faucet off and replaced the hose. He watered the roses carefully after dinner ended.

The ticket printer jammed at the airport check in desk. A clerk removed the wrinkled paper with patient hands. She reset the machine behind the counter and waited. My boarding pass printed correctly on the next try.

Red paint splashed across my shirt sleeve during class. I rinsed the fabric under cold water immediately. I rubbed soap into the stain with careful fingers. The stain faded before the paint could fully dry.

The fence gate squeaked loudly every morning after sunrise. Grandpa sprayed oil onto the metal hinges and latch. He pushed the gate back and forth several times. The gate swung quietly after only a single minute.

The music teacher tapped a steady rhythm on the desk. The class repeated the pattern together on small drums. One student finally matched the beat exactly and clearly. The teacher nodded and smiled with real pride.

A branch fell across the forest path after strong wind. Hikers stopped beside the blocked trail and looked around. They dragged the heavy branch into the bushes together. They continued uphill toward the lake before sunset.

A child stacked bright blocks into a tall tower carefully. The top block leaned slowly toward one side. The tower collapsed across the carpet with a sudden crash. She built a wider base and tried again.
Our paper map got soaked in rain near the station. We stood under an awning beside a closed shop. The pages dried slowly in the warm afternoon breeze. We found the platform and caught the next train.

The stove would not light when dinner time finally came. Dad checked the gas knob and the pilot carefully. He relit the pilot with a match from the drawer. Mom cooked the vegetables once the flame returned steadily.

I left my wallet at the crowded cafe downtown. I hurried back along the same street and crossed quickly. The cashier held it safely behind the counter. I thanked her and walked home feeling deeply relieved.

The sandcastle looked tall and detailed near the shoreline. Waves crept higher across the beach with every minute. A cold splash knocked down the front wall completely. The children laughed and started building a new one.

The classroom projector failed just before the lesson began. Mrs Lee closed the blinds halfway and changed her plan. She moved the students closer to the board. She taught the lesson with chalk and clear diagrams.
A tomato slipped from the cutting board after I chopped. It rolled under the kitchen table beside one chair. I knelt down, found it, and picked it up. I washed it again and finished the salad.

The neighbor's dog barked outside the window all evening. Mina closed the windows facing the small back yard. She turned on soft music in the living room. She finished her book peacefully before bedtime arrived.

The dresser drawer jammed halfway while Ian searched for shirts. He removed the clothes and inspected the metal rail. He realigned the loose track with a small screwdriver. The drawer opened smoothly after the quick repair.
A storm started while we were grilling in the yard. We covered the grill and closed the metal lid. We carried the chairs into the garage together. We watched the rain from the porch after that.
A paper airplane struck the ceiling fan above the sofa. It spun away and dropped behind the couch cushions. Max adjusted his throw and aimed much lower. The next airplane landed neatly on the rug.
My sweater shrank after a mistake in the washing machine. I soaked it in cool water near the sink. I stretched the fabric gently with both hands. I dried it flat beside the sunny window.

The chess clock stopped during our long game in silence. Ravi opened the back and checked the battery. He replaced it with a fresh one from home. We restarted the game and recorded the moves carefully.
The toothpaste cap slipped into the sink after brushing. I reached down twice but missed the cap. I lifted it out with a pair of tweezers. I rinsed the cap and closed the tube again.
The pond froze during the coldest night of winter. We laced our skates beside the empty wooden bench. Dad tested the ice near the edge carefully. We glided slowly across the clear frozen surface.
Steam covered the kitchen window while Mia cooked noodles. She turned on the vent above the stove quickly. She wiped the glass with a dry towel. She could see the street clearly again from inside.

The flashlight beam grew weak in the dark cellar. I opened the case and removed the old batteries. I inserted fresh batteries from the kitchen drawer upstairs. The bright beam reached every corner again that night.

A grocery bag tore near the handle outside the store. Several oranges rolled across the parking lot in different directions. I gathered them quickly and found a stronger bag. I reached home without losing any of the fruit.

The campfire smoked heavily when Liam first lit the wood. He rearranged the damp logs with a metal stick. Fresh air moved through the center gap between them. Bright flames rose and warmed our cold hands.

My theater seat leaned backward strangely during the previews. I told an usher and pointed toward the broken hinge. She moved me to an empty row near the aisle. I watched the film comfortably from that seat.
The plant leaves drooped over the edge of the pot. I pressed the dry soil with one finger. I watered the plant until drops appeared below. The leaves lifted again by the next morning.
The cereal box slipped from the high kitchen shelf. Ava caught one corner before the box could fall. She steadied it against her arm and chest. She poured breakfast into a bowl and added milk.

The mailbox jammed with thick envelopes and small advertisements. I pulled the stack out slowly with both hands. I sorted the bills from the colorful flyers outside. I recycled the flyers before walking back indoors.

A violin string snapped during practice in the afternoon. Nora opened her case and found a spare string. She fitted the string carefully onto the pegs. She tuned the violin and resumed playing scales.

The coffee grinder clogged with oily beans this morning. I brushed the chamber with a narrow cleaning tool. Loose grounds fell into the tray below the blades. I brewed a stronger cup after the grinder cleared.
My paintbrush dried stiff after art class ended. I soaked the bristles in warm water by the sink. I washed out the remaining blue pigment slowly. The brush softened and bent normally once again.

The elevator stopped between two floors without warning. We pressed the alarm button and waited silently. An operator spoke through the speaker in a calm voice. The doors opened after a technician finally arrived.

A recipe card blew from the counter near the bowl. It slid under the kitchen table and disappeared briefly. I trapped it with a mixing bowl before it moved. I read the steps and continued cooking dinner.

My shirt looked wrinkled before the important morning meeting. I sprayed the collar and sleeves with water. I ironed the front with slow, steady strokes. I folded the shirt and packed it carefully afterward.
The robot vacuum caught the rug fringe near the chair. Its wheels spun uselessly and made a whining sound. I lifted the rug edge and freed the wheels. The vacuum resumed its path across the room.
A ceramic bowl cracked in the freezer during the night. Soup leaked onto the shelf and dripped below. I moved the soup into a metal pot. I warmed lunch on the stove before noon.
The rowing boat drifted from the dock in silence. Eva grabbed the loose rope and pulled hard. She brought the boat back with both hands. She tied a tighter knot around the wooden post.

The library computer restarted without warning during my essay. My screen went black and the cursor vanished. I reopened the document from the backup folder. I finished the last paragraph before closing time.
My sunglasses fell into dry sand near the path. I shook the frames over the grass several times. I wiped the lenses with the cleanest part of my shirt. I wore them again during the long walk.

The tent zipper caught the fabric flap before sunset. Noah backed the slider up slowly and checked it. He freed the cloth with careful fingers and patience. He closed the flap before the rain started.
A wall clock showed the wrong time above the stove. I opened the back and changed the batteries. I turned the hands to the correct hour. I caught the train after all that morning.

The classroom marker ran dry in the middle of the lesson. Mr Patel opened a new marker from the box. He wrote the equation clearly across the board. The students copied the notes quietly into notebooks.

The bicycle chain slipped off during a turn downhill. Rosa stopped pedaling beside the curb and looked down. She lifted the chain back onto the gear carefully. She rode slowly to the repair shop after that.
The noodle pot boiled nearly dry on the stove. I added water and stirred the noodles quickly. I lowered the heat under the burner at once. Dinner turned out fine a few minutes later.
A package label peeled up at one corner today. I pressed it flat, but it lifted again. I wrapped clear tape around the box carefully. The parcel reached the post office in good shape.
My shoe sole came loose in front during rain. I cleaned the gap with a tissue and towel. I spread glue along the rubber edge evenly. I wore the shoe again after it dried overnight.

The router blinked red during the online team meeting. Ana restarted the modem under her desk and waited. She watched the signal lights stabilize one by one. She joined the call before introductions were finished.

The bird feeder hung empty beside the porch this morning. Dad carried out a bucket full of sunflower seeds. He filled the feeder and brushed away old shells. Sparrows arrived before he stepped back inside the house.
My umbrella turned inside out in the strong wind. I stepped into a shop doorway and closed it. I straightened the ribs and fixed the frame. I kept walking once the heavy rain eased.
My scarf slipped from my shoulder bag near the crossing. I noticed the loss after passing three small shops. I retraced my route and searched every doorway. I found the scarf resting on a bench.

The bathroom mirror fogged after my hot morning shower. I opened the vent and the bathroom door wide. I wiped the glass carefully with a clean towel. I shaved slowly in the clear reflection afterward.

The oven timer rang loudly across the quiet kitchen. I pulled the tray out while wearing thick mitts. Steam rose from the hot blueberry muffins at once. I cooled them briefly and served breakfast right away.

The doorknob rattled every time Leah opened the door. Leah removed the cover plate with a small screwdriver. She tightened the loose screws hidden underneath it. The handle felt firm and quiet after the repair.
The kite tail tore during the crowded summer fair. Emma tied a ribbon around the rip with care. She lifted the string into a stronger breeze. The kite climbed high above the colorful tents.
A bottle rolled off the picnic blanket on the hill. It bumped down the grass toward the narrow stream. Amir chased it before it reached the water. He brought it back and closed the cap tightly.
The shop sign flickered above the door after dusk. The owner climbed a ladder carrying a box of tools. He replaced the loose bulb inside the sign. The sign glowed brightly for customers after sunset.
A jar lid would not open during the busy cooking hour. Mira wrapped it with a dry towel for grip. She twisted hard with both hands and shoulders. The lid popped loose with a sharp sound.
The printer tray held the wrong paper for the contract. I removed the thick colored sheets from the tray. I loaded plain white pages into the printer. The contract printed clearly on the next try.

A loose tile rocked under my foot in the hallway. I crouched down and inspected the lifted corner carefully. I pressed fresh adhesive beneath the tile with a tool. It stayed flat by the afternoon without moving.
The camp lantern dimmed after midnight beside the tents. Noah turned the knob and checked the fuel level. He added fuel from a small metal can. The lantern glowed brighter for the rest of the night.

The horse stopped at a fallen log on the trail. The rider guided it toward a lower place nearby. The horse stepped over the obstacle with calm strength. They continued along the forest trail without trouble.

A stack of plates leaned dangerously high inside the cabinet. Jenna moved two plates to the lower shelf below. The stack settled into a safer position at once. She closed the cabinet without any trouble afterward.
noncausal_texts: A violin is played with a bow. A flute is played by blowing air. A drum is played by striking it. A harp is played by plucking strings.
Honey is made by bees from flower nectar. Honey can be clear, amber, or dark brown. Honey is used in tea, bread, and sauces. Honey contains mostly natural sugars and very little water.

A camel has long legs for walking across hot sand. A camel stores fat in its hump, not water. A camel can close its nostrils during dusty desert winds. A camel often carries people or goods across dry regions.

A volcano opens through cracks in the Earth's rocky crust. A volcano can release lava, ash, and hot gases. A volcano often forms a cone around repeated eruptions. A volcano may stand alone or along mountain chains.

A bicycle moves on two wheels connected by a frame. A bicycle uses pedals, chains, and gears for motion. A bicycle handlebar helps riders steer through narrow spaces. A bicycle is common in cities and quiet villages.

A maple tree often has broad leaves with pointed lobes. A maple tree produces winged seeds called samaras. A maple tree can turn bright red or orange. A maple tree grows in parks, streets, and forests.

The Moon circles Earth and reflects sunlight at night. The Moon has many craters, plains, and dusty hills. The Moon causes noticeable changes in ocean tides on Earth. The Moon has no weather like wind or rain.

A library stores books, magazines, maps, and other materials. A library often provides quiet tables for reading or study. A library card allows borrowing in many public systems. A library may host talks, clubs, or language classes.

A coral reef is built by tiny marine animals called polyps. A coral reef supports fish, crabs, turtles, and many algae. A coral reef usually grows in warm, shallow seawater. A coral reef can form long barriers near tropical coasts.

Chess is played on a board with sixty four squares. Chess uses six kinds of pieces with different moves. Chess requires planning, calculation, and careful attention to threats. Chess can end with checkmate, stalemate, or resignation.

Rice grows well in warm fields with plenty of water. Rice is eaten steamed, fried, or ground into flour. Rice grains can be white, brown, red, or black. Rice feeds millions of people across many different countries.

A telescope collects light from distant stars and planets. A telescope can use mirrors, lenses, or both together. A telescope helps astronomers study objects too faint for eyes. A telescope may stand on mountains or orbit above Earth.

A penguin has flippers instead of wings for flight. A penguin swims quickly through cold water with strong strokes. A penguin often nests in large groups on land. A penguin usually eats fish, squid, or small crustaceans.

A magnet pulls iron and some other metals toward itself. A magnet has north and south poles with opposite behavior. A magnet can be natural stone or manufactured material. A magnet is used in speakers, motors, and compasses.

A lighthouse stands near a dangerous coast or busy harbor. A lighthouse sends bright flashes across dark water at night. A lighthouse helps ships identify shorelines, rocks, and channels. A lighthouse tower is usually tall, narrow, and highly visible.

Bamboo grows quickly and has hollow stems with hard joints. Bamboo belongs to the grass family, not the tree family. Bamboo is used for flooring, baskets, paper, and food. Bamboo leaves are narrow, light, and often sharply pointed.

A glacier is a huge mass of compacted moving ice. A glacier forms where snow gathers faster than it melts. A glacier can carve valleys and carry rocky debris. A glacier usually moves slowly down a mountain slope.

Olive oil is pressed from the fruit of olive trees. Olive oil can taste grassy, peppery, fruity, or mild. Olive oil is common in dressings, sauces, and roasting. Olive oil contains mostly unsaturated fat rather than solid fat.

An octopus has eight arms lined with strong suckers. An octopus can change color and skin texture quickly. An octopus has no bones inside its flexible body. An octopus lives in seas from shallow reefs to deep water.

An umbrella has a folding frame covered with waterproof fabric. An umbrella protects people from rain and strong sunlight. An umbrella is carried by a handle or short shaft. An umbrella can be small enough for a shoulder bag.

Cinnamon comes from the inner bark of several tree species. Cinnamon has a warm smell and a sweet spicy taste. Cinnamon is used in baked goods, drinks, and savory dishes. Cinnamon can be sold as sticks or ground powder.

A sunflower has a large flower head with many tiny florets. A sunflower turns fields bright yellow during the blooming season. A sunflower produces edible seeds rich in oil and flavor. A sunflower usually grows on a tall, rough stem.

A microscope makes very small objects appear much larger. A microscope often uses glass lenses and a bright light. A microscope helps scientists study cells, fibers, and crystals. A microscope can stand on a desk inside a laboratory.

A dolphin breathes air through an opening on its head. A dolphin uses sound to find objects and other animals. A dolphin lives in oceans and some large rivers. A dolphin often travels in social groups called pods.

A passport is an official document for international travel. A passport contains identification details, a photograph, and blank pages. A passport is checked at borders, airports, and consulates. A passport cover color varies across different countries.

Marble is a stone changed by heat and pressure underground. Marble can be white, green, black, or pink. Marble is used for floors, statues, counters, and walls. Marble often shows smooth surfaces with veins and swirls.

A thunderstorm brings lightning, thunder, rain, and gusty winds. A thunderstorm forms inside tall clouds with rising moisture. A thunderstorm can appear in summer afternoons or winter fronts. A thunderstorm may produce hail, flooding, or dangerous strikes.
Tea is made by steeping leaves in hot water. Tea can taste grassy, floral, smoky, or earthy. Tea is served plain, sweetened, milky, or iced. Tea comes in green, black, white, and oolong varieties.

A windmill uses moving air to turn large blades. A windmill can pump water or grind grain. A windmill usually stands in open land with steady breezes. A windmill has appeared in farming regions for many centuries.

A strawberry has tiny seeds on the outside surface. A strawberry tastes sweet, tart, and fragrant when ripe. A strawberry can be eaten fresh or cooked into jam. A strawberry plant stays low and spreads with runners.

A train station has platforms, tracks, signs, and waiting areas. A train station may contain ticket machines, shops, and benches. A train station connects neighborhoods, suburbs, airports, or distant cities. A train station can be underground or open to weather.

An elephant has a long trunk with many useful muscles. An elephant uses tusks for digging, lifting, and defense. An elephant has large ears that release excess body heat. An elephant lives in herds led by experienced females.

A compass points toward magnetic north when held level. A compass helps travelers choose direction without visible landmarks. A compass has a needle, markings, and a clear face. A compass remains useful when batteries or signals are unavailable.

A snowflake forms when water vapor freezes in clouds. A snowflake often shows six sided symmetry with fine branches. A snowflake changes shape under different temperatures and humidity. A snowflake is delicate enough to melt on warm skin.

A brick is made from clay, shale, or similar material. A brick is used in walls, chimneys, paths, and ovens. A brick can be red, brown, gray, or cream. A brick usually has a rectangular shape and flat faces.

A notebook holds blank or lined pages for writing. A notebook may contain sketches, notes, formulas, or lists. A notebook can use spiral wire, staples, or sewn binding. A notebook fits easily into many bags or desks.

A cactus stores water inside thick stems and tissues. A cactus often grows spines instead of broad leaves. A cactus thrives in dry places with strong sunlight. A cactus flower can be bright, brief, and striking.

A waterfall is a place where water drops steeply downward. A waterfall may cut rock and create misty spray below. A waterfall can be narrow, broad, tall, or terraced. A waterfall often becomes a landmark in forests or parks.

Silk is a smooth fiber made by silkworm caterpillars. Silk can feel cool, soft, light, and slightly shiny. Silk is used for clothing, scarves, ties, and bedding. Silk accepts dyes well and often shows rich colors.

A pumpkin has thick skin, firm flesh, and many seeds. A pumpkin can be orange, white, green, or striped. A pumpkin is cooked in soups, pies, and roasted dishes. A pumpkin grows on a spreading vine with large leaves.

A satellite travels around Earth or another celestial body. A satellite may carry cameras, antennas, sensors, or solar panels. A satellite supports communication, navigation, weather reports, and research. A satellite can be launched by rockets from several countries.

A peacock is a large bird with a fan shaped tail. A peacock belongs to the pheasant family of birds. A peacock has feathers that show bright blue and bronze colors. A peacock lives on the ground and roosts in trees.

A staircase connects one level of a building to another. A staircase may be straight, curved, narrow, or wide. A staircase uses steps, railings, and landings for movement. A staircase can be made from wood, stone, or steel.

A lemon has bright yellow skin and sour juice. A lemon is used in drinks, desserts, dressings, and sauces. A lemon tree has glossy leaves and fragrant flowers. A lemon adds sharp flavor to fish, vegetables, and cakes.

A lantern surrounds a light source with a protective frame. A lantern can use candles, oil, gas, or batteries. A lantern is useful during camping, travel, and power outages. A lantern often has a handle for carrying or hanging.

A panda has black patches around its eyes and ears. A panda eats bamboo, shoots, leaves, and some other plants. A panda has strong jaws for crushing tough stems. A panda spends much of the day eating or resting.

Coffee is brewed from roasted and ground coffee beans. Coffee can taste nutty, fruity, chocolatey, or smoky. Coffee is served black, milky, iced, or sweetened. Coffee contains caffeine unless it has been decaffeinated.
A desert receives very little rain during a year. A desert can be rocky, sandy, cold, or high. Desert plants often have deep roots or water storage. Desert animals are active at dawn, night, or dusk.

A feather covers birds and helps with warmth or flight. A feather has a central shaft with many small branches. A feather can be soft, stiff, striped, or iridescent. A feather is much lighter than a leaf of similar size.

A castle is a fortified building with thick defensive walls. A castle may include towers, gates, courtyards, and great halls. A castle often stands on hills, rivers, or strategic roads. A castle can serve military, political, or residential purposes.

Garlic grows as a bulb made of separate cloves. Garlic has a strong smell and a sharp taste. Garlic is used in sauces, soups, breads, and roasted dishes. Garlic leaves are long, flat, and green before harvest.

A river carries fresh water downhill toward another body. A river can curve across plains or cut through canyons. A river supports fish, birds, farms, and human settlements. A river may begin from rain, lakes, or mountain snow.

Clay is a fine soil material that becomes sticky when wet. Clay can be shaped into pots, bricks, tiles, and sculptures. Clay hardens when dried or fired at high heat. Clay colors include red, gray, brown, and white.

A kangaroo moves with strong hind legs and a long tail. A kangaroo carries young animals inside a pouch. A kangaroo lives mainly in Australia and nearby islands. A kangaroo can stand upright and balance while resting.

A calendar shows days, weeks, months, and named holidays. A calendar can be printed on paper or displayed digitally. A calendar helps people plan meetings, travel, and deadlines. A calendar may start weeks on Sunday or Monday.

An iceberg is a large floating mass of freshwater ice. An iceberg breaks away from glaciers or ice shelves. An iceberg shows only a small part above water. An iceberg can drift for months in cold ocean currents.
A walnut has a hard shell and a wrinkled kernel. A walnut can be eaten raw, toasted, or baked. A walnut tree has broad leaves and rough bark. A walnut adds rich flavor to bread, salads, and desserts.

A fountain pen stores liquid ink inside a reservoir or cartridge. A fountain pen writes through a metal nib and slit. A fountain pen can produce line variation with pressure or angle. A fountain pen is popular with many handwriting enthusiasts.

A spider web is made from silk produced by spiders. A spider web can trap flying insects with sticky threads. A spider web may look circular, tangled, flat, or funnel shaped. A spider web often sparkles with dew in morning light.

A cathedral is a large church with a bishop's official seat. A cathedral may include arches, towers, stained glass, and carvings. A cathedral often serves worship, music, ceremonies, and public gatherings. A cathedral can stand at the center of an old city.

Yogurt is made by fermenting milk with helpful bacteria. Yogurt can be plain, sweet, thick, or drinkable. Yogurt is eaten with fruit, grains, herbs, or honey. Yogurt provides protein, calcium, and a tangy flavor.

A hurricane is a large rotating storm over warm ocean water. A hurricane has strong winds, heavy rain, and spiral bands. A hurricane is given a personal name during each season. A hurricane can cause flooding along coasts and inland areas.

A cedar tree often has aromatic wood and evergreen needles. A cedar tree can grow tall and live many years. Cedar wood resists insects and weather reasonably well. A cedar tree is used for closets, chests, and buildings.

A hammer has a handle and a weighted striking head. A hammer is used for nails, shaping, and light demolition. A hammer head may be steel, stone, or another hard material. A hammer feels heavier at one end than the other.

A jellyfish has a soft body with trailing tentacles. A jellyfish moves by pulsing water through its bell. A jellyfish can drift with currents in seas worldwide. A jellyfish may glow faintly or sting with tiny cells.

A rainbow appears as colored bands in water droplets. A rainbow usually shows red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet. A rainbow is seen opposite the Sun from an observer. A rainbow can look faint, bright, broad, or doubled.

A quilt is a blanket made from layered fabric sections. A quilt may contain patterns, stitching, batting, and borders. A quilt can be handmade or sewn by machine. A quilt provides warmth and decoration on beds or walls.
An apricot is a small fruit with orange flesh. An apricot has a smooth skin and a single stone. An apricot can be dried, cooked, or eaten fresh. An apricot tree blooms with pale flowers in spring.

A subway carries many passengers through crowded urban areas. A subway often runs underground but sometimes rises outdoors. A subway uses electric trains, platforms, tunnels, and signs. A subway helps reduce road traffic in large cities.
A koala has thick fur, round ears, and a broad nose. A koala eats eucalyptus leaves for most of its diet. A koala sleeps many hours in tree branches. A koala climbs with sharp claws and strong limbs.

A mirror reflects light and forms an image of objects. A mirror may be flat, curved, framed, or handheld. A mirror is used in bedrooms, cars, stores, and bathrooms. A mirror surface often uses glass coated with metal.

Moss is a small plant without true flowers or seeds. Moss grows well on damp soil, stone, bark, and roofs. Moss stays low and forms soft green carpets. Moss absorbs water directly across much of its surface.

A camera captures light to create still images or video. A camera can use film, digital sensors, or both. A camera lens changes focus, framing, and brightness. A camera may be small enough for pockets or large for studios.

An igloo is a dome shaped shelter built from snow blocks. An igloo is used in very cold regions with packed snow. An igloo traps warmth effectively because snow contains insulating air. An igloo entrance is often lower than the sleeping area.
A pear has juicy flesh and a narrow neck shape. A pear can be crisp, soft, green, yellow, or red. A pear is eaten fresh, poached, baked, or sliced. A pear tree blooms with white flowers in spring.

A comet is a small icy body moving around the Sun. A comet can develop a glowing coma and long tail. A comet tail points away from the Sun because of radiation. A comet may return after years, centuries, or never again.
A sailboat moves with wind pushing against its sails. A sailboat has a hull, mast, ropes, and rudder. A sailboat can be small enough for one person. A sailboat may travel on lakes, rivers, or oceans.

A tortoise has a heavy shell and stout legs. A tortoise lives on land rather than in water. A tortoise eats grasses, leaves, flowers, and some fruit. A tortoise can live for many decades in safe conditions.

Bread is made from flour, water, and usually yeast. Bread can be flat, round, sliced, crusty, or soft. Bread is baked in ovens and served worldwide. Bread varieties include sourdough, rye, brioche, and many others.

An accordion is a musical instrument with bellows and keys. An accordion produces sound when air moves past reeds. An accordion can play melody, harmony, and rhythm together. An accordion is common in folk music from many regions.

Lavender has narrow leaves and small purple flower spikes. Lavender has a strong scent used in soap and perfume. Lavender grows well in sunny soil with good drainage. Lavender can be dried for sachets, tea, or decoration.

An anchor is a heavy device used to hold boats. An anchor grips the bottom with flukes, weight, or shape. An anchor is attached by chain, rope, or both together. An anchor often represents stability, safety, and hope.

A beehive is a home built by bees for a colony. A beehive contains wax cells for brood, pollen, and honey. A beehive can hang from branches or stand inside boxes. A beehive is warm, busy, and highly organized inside.

Granite is an igneous rock formed from cooled magma. Granite often contains visible crystals of quartz and feldspar. Granite is used for countertops, monuments, buildings, and paving. Granite can appear pink, gray, black, or speckled white.

A parrot has a curved beak and strong gripping feet. A parrot often shows bright feathers in green, blue, red, or yellow. A parrot can crack seeds, nuts, and fruit with ease. A parrot lives in tropical forests, savannas, and homes.

A faucet controls water flow through a sink or pipe. A faucet may have one handle or separate hot and cold handles. A faucet can be made from brass, steel, or plastic. A faucet is common in kitchens, bathrooms, and gardens.

A monastery is a religious community where monks or nuns live. A monastery may include gardens, workshops, libraries, and quiet rooms. A monastery often values prayer, study, labor, and shared meals. A monastery can stand in cities, mountains, or deserts.

A carrot is a root vegetable with crisp sweet flesh. A carrot can be orange, purple, yellow, red, or white. A carrot is eaten raw, roasted, steamed, or grated. A carrot grows underground beneath feathery green leaves.

A roller skate has wheels attached below a boot. A roller skate allows movement on smooth floors or pavement. A roller skate can use two front wheels and two back wheels. A roller skate is used for sport, dance, and recreation.

A geyser sends hot water and steam high into the air. A geyser forms where underground water meets hot rock. Geyser eruptions can be frequent, rare, tall, or short. A geyser often occurs in volcanic or geothermal regions.

Sapphire is a hard gemstone usually associated with blue color. Sapphire can also appear pink, yellow, green, or colorless. Sapphire is used in jewelry, watches, and some instruments. Sapphire belongs to the mineral family called corundum.

A beaver has large teeth and a broad flat tail. A beaver cuts branches and builds dams across streams. A beaver lives in ponds, wetlands, and forest waterways. A beaver has thick fur that resists cold water.

A postcard is a small card for messages and pictures. A postcard usually shows a scene, city, artwork, or joke. A postcard can be mailed without an envelope in many places. A postcard is often collected as a simple souvenir.

A fern is a leafy plant that reproduces with spores. A fern has fronds that uncurl from tight young coils. A fern often grows in moist forests, gardens, or shaded rocks. A fern can be tiny, feathery, or tree sized.

A wind chime makes sound when moving parts strike together. A wind chime can be made from metal, bamboo, glass, or shells. A wind chime hangs from porches, trees, windows, or garden hooks. A wind chime produces tones that vary with size and shape.

A canyon is a deep valley with steep rock walls. A canyon is carved by rivers, erosion, uplift, or time. A canyon can show colorful layers from different geologic periods. A canyon often contains trails, cliffs, and dramatic viewpoints.

An almond is the edible seed inside a hard shell. An almond can be eaten raw, roasted, sliced, or ground. An almond tree blooms with pale flowers before leaf growth. An almond is used in desserts, milk, and savory dishes.

An hourglass measures time with sand in two bulbs. An hourglass uses gravity to move grains through a narrow neck. An hourglass can be small enough for a desk. An hourglass is often used as a symbol of passing time.

A thermometer measures temperature using expansion, electricity, or infrared sensing. A thermometer can show Celsius, Fahrenheit, or both scales. A thermometer is used in homes, clinics, kitchens, and laboratories. A thermometer may be digital, glass, or noncontact in design.

A thermos has double walls that reduce heat transfer. A thermos keeps drinks hot or cold for hours. A thermos usually has a lid that seals tightly. A thermos is useful for travel, work, school, and camping.
